agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A courier-tracking feature is proposed to show customers their courier's real-time location during delivery. Two internal stakeholder teams have submitted directly contradictory requirements: Customer Success demands full GPS transparency as a competitive differentiator, while Operations demands location never be shown due to driver retention concerns. This is a fundamental business conflict that cannot be resolved by engineering — it requires executive-level arbitration.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Courier Visibility","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Courier","definition":"A delivery driver contracted or employed to transport packages from origin to customer destination."},{"term":"Real-time location","definition":"GPS coordinates of the courier's device, updated at frequent intervals during an active delivery."},{"term":"Active delivery","definition":"The time window between a courier picking up a package and completing delivery to the customer."},{"term":"Delivery transparency","definition":"The degree to which delivery progress and courier position are visible to the end customer."},{"term":"Driver retention","definition":"The ability to keep couriers working on the platform; a critical supply-side metric in courier logistics."}]},"business_rules":[],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Courier opts out of sharing location but customer expects visibility","expected_behavior":"Cannot define — depends on resolution of the core contradiction."},{"scenario":"Courier is stationary for extended period (e.g., traffic, break) and customer sees no movement","expected_behavior":"Exact GPS exposes idle time which may generate complaints; approximate ETA-based tracking avoids this. Resolution depends on which approach is chosen."},{"scenario":"Courier is near customer's home but delivering to a neighbor or multi-unit building","expected_behavior":"Exact GPS near a residential address raises privacy and safety concerns for both courier and customer."},{"scenario":"Courier is multi-dropping (delivering to multiple customers on one route)","expected_behavior":"Showing exact location to Customer A reveals that courier is detouring to Customer B, creating frustration. This is a known pain point in courier logistics."},{"scenario":"Off-duty or between-delivery location tracking","expected_behavior":"Must never expose courier location outside of an active delivery window regardless of resolution — this is a baseline privacy invariant."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Both sides carry significant financial risk. Losing driver supply (Operations concern) directly impacts delivery capacity, SLAs, and revenue. Losing customer satisfaction (Customer Success concern) impacts retention and acquisition. A two-sided marketplace cannot optimize for one side at the expense of the other without existential risk.","operational":"Driver churn increases recruitment and onboarding costs (typically $500-2000 per driver in logistics). If drivers leave due to surveillance concerns, delivery capacity drops, SLAs degrade, and remaining drivers are overloaded — creating a death spiral. Conversely, removing all visibility may increase 'where is my package' support volume significantly.","user":"Customers expect some form of delivery visibility — this is table stakes in modern logistics (set by Uber, DoorDash, Amazon). However, 'exact GPS coordinates at all times' is far beyond what most competitors actually provide. Most use approximate location or ETA-based progress. Couriers are stakeholders too — their sense of autonomy and dignity directly affects service quality.","risk":"CRITICAL: Implementing either requirement as stated creates legal exposure. Exact GPS tracking of workers may violate labor privacy regulations in multiple jurisdictions (GDPR Article 88, various US state laws). Showing zero tracking information is commercially uncompetitive. Both extremes are high-risk positions.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Desire delivery visibility; exact GPS is more than most expect but removal of all tracking would be a step backward from industry standard."},{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"Directly affected — exact GPS creates surveillance pressure, affects autonomy, and is cited as a retention driver. Their welfare is a supply-side business constraint."},{"group":"Customer Success team","impact":"KPIs likely tied to CSAT and NPS which they believe tracking improves. Need to validate whether 'exact GPS' is actually what drives satisfaction or if approximate tracking suffices."},{"group":"Operations team","impact":"Owns driver retention metrics. Losing drivers is an existential threat to a courier business. Their concern is grounded in reported driver feedback."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"NOT YET CONSULTED — this is a gap. Worker GPS tracking has regulatory implications in many jurisdictions that neither team has addressed."},{"group":"Executive leadership","impact":"Must arbitrate this contradiction. This is a strategic product decision about marketplace balance, not a feature spec decision."}]},"confidence":{"level":30,"reasoning":"Confidence is critically low because the spec contains a direct, unresolvable contradiction between two non-negotiable requirements from equally authoritative stakeholder teams. No acceptance criteria can be defined until this is resolved at an executive level. Additionally, Legal/Compliance has not been consulted on what is a clear worker-privacy question, which could invalidate either position. The spec as written is not implementable.","high_confidence_areas":["This is a genuine business contradiction, not a technical misunderstanding","Courier location must NEVER be exposed outside active delivery windows — both sides would agree on this baseline","Industry standard is approximate tracking (ETA-based or zone-based), not exact GPS — neither extreme position reflects market norms","Legal review is required before any implementation"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which stakeholder team's requirement should take priority","Whether a middle-ground solution (e.g., approximate location, ETA-only, courier opt-in) would satisfy both teams","The actual magnitude of driver attrition caused by tracking vs. other factors","Whether Customer Success has data proving exact GPS drives satisfaction, or if this is assumed"],"assumptions":["Both teams are operating in good faith with real business concerns","Driver retention complaints about surveillance are based on actual driver feedback, not hypothetical","Customer Success has not validated whether 'exact GPS' specifically (vs. approximate tracking) is the differentiator","No legal review of worker GPS tracking obligations has been conducted"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Customer Success requires courier GPS to always be visible to customers. Operations requires courier GPS to never be visible to customers. These are mutually exclusive requirements from teams with equal authority. This cannot be resolved at the engineering level — it is a strategic product decision that requires executive arbitration.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Couriers","Customer Success team","Operations team","Executive leadership"],"options":["A: Side with Customer Success — show exact GPS (risk: driver attrition, potential labor law violations)","B: Side with Operations — show no location (risk: commercially uncompetitive, increased support volume)","C: Executive-mediated compromise — approximate/zone-based tracking with courier consent model (recommended industry approach)","D: Defer feature entirely pending legal review of worker GPS tracking obligations"],"recommendation":"Option C with Option D as a prerequisite. The industry-standard approach is approximate tracking (zone-based or ETA-based), not exact GPS. This likely satisfies customer needs without creating a surveillance environment. However, NO implementation should proceed until Legal/Compliance reviews worker location-sharing obligations in all operating jurisdictions. Recommend executive working session with both teams plus Legal to define a unified policy."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"Legal and compliance review of courier GPS tracking has not been conducted. In many jurisdictions (EU under GDPR, California under CCPA, Illinois under BIPA, etc.), tracking worker location and sharing it with third parties has specific legal requirements including consent, data minimization, and purpose limitation. This is not optional context — it could invalidate either team's position entirely.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance","Couriers","Executive leadership"],"options":["Engage legal counsel before defining any requirements","Proceed with implementation and retrofit compliance later (NOT recommended)"],"recommendation":"Engage legal counsel immediately. Do not write a single line of code until legal boundaries are established. Compliance violations in worker tracking carry severe penalties."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Direct contradiction between two authoritative stakeholder requirements makes the spec unimplementable as written.","business_rule_broken":"No coherent business rule can be derived when one team says 'always show' and another says 'never show'. A business rule must be singular and unambiguous."},{"violation":"No legal/compliance review of worker GPS tracking has been conducted.","business_rule_broken":"Any feature involving sharing worker location data with third parties must be reviewed for regulatory compliance before requirements are finalized. This is a baseline governance invariant in logistics."}]}
